entry.title: SAP PO GraphQL Integration with XSLT Mapping
entry.content:
Introduction:

In most SAP projects, integrations are built using REST or OData. But in some cases, modern systems require GraphQL (Graph Query Language), a query language and runtime for APIs that allows clients to request exactly the data they need. When calling a GraphQL service from SAP PO, the service expects application/json, which means we need a transformation step. Using XSLT mapping, we converted the XML request into the JSON format GraphQL understands and executed the call successfully. In this post, I’ll walk you through how we set this up and what we learned along the way.

Note: In this scenario, no authentication was required since the GraphQL service was internal and not exposed externally. However, if the service had been publicly accessible, common security mechanisms such as OAuth 2.0, JWT tokens, or API Keys would typically be implemented. On the SAP PO side, this would be handled by configuring the HTTP/REST adapter to pass the necessary headers (e.g., Authorization: Bearer <token>), ensuring secure communication.

 

Scenario:

Request Example:

Response Example:

 

Step 1: Create Data Type, Message Type , Service Interfaces

Data Type Request:

For this scenario, I defined a dummy field named query. The GraphQL request is embedded into this field and then passed to the target service.

Data Type Response:

You can either create the response message as a Data Type (DT) or convert it into an XSD and import it into PO as an External Definition. I converted it into an XSD and imported it into PO as an External Definition.

Message Type Request :

 

Service Interfaces: 

We also create our Service Interfaces — one inbound and one outbound — and, as usual, place our request and response messages accordingly.

 

 

Step 2: XSLT Mapping 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>

<xsl:stylesheet version="1.0" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform">

  <xsl:output method="text" encoding="UTF-8"/>

  <xsl:template match="/">

  <xsl:variable name="q1" select="string(/*/*[local-name()='query'])"/>

    <xsl:variable name="q">

      <xsl:choose>

        <xsl:when test="string-length($q1) > 0">

          <xsl:value-of select="$q1"/>

        </xsl:when>

        <xsl:otherwise>

          <xsl:value-of select="string(/*)"/>

        </xsl:otherwise>

      </xsl:choose>

    </xsl:variable>

    <xsl:text>{ "query":"</xsl:text>

    <xsl:call-template name="escape-json">

      <xsl:with-param name="s" select="string($q)"/>

    </xsl:call-template>

    <xsl:text>" }</xsl:text>

  </xsl:template>

  <!-- JSON escape: \  "  LF  CR  TAB -->

  <xsl:template name="escape-json">

    <xsl:param name="s"/>

    <!-- \ -> \\ -->

    <xsl:variable name="step1">

      <xsl:call-template name="replace">

        <xsl:with-param name="text" select="$s"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="search" select="'\'"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="repl" select="'\\'"/>

      </xsl:call-template>

    </xsl:variable>

    <!-- " -> \" (XML için " kullan) -->

    <xsl:variable name="step2">

      <xsl:call-template name="replace">

        <xsl:with-param name="text"   select="string($step1)"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="search" select="'"'"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="repl"   select="'\"'"/>

      </xsl:call-template>

    </xsl:variable>

    <!-- LF, CR, TAB -->

    <xsl:variable name="step3">

      <xsl:call-template name="replace">

        <xsl:with-param name="text" select="string($step2)"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="search" select="'
'"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="repl" select="'\n'"/>

      </xsl:call-template>

    </xsl:variable>

    <xsl:variable name="step4">

      <xsl:call-template name="replace">

        <xsl:with-param name="text" select="string($step3)"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="search" select="''"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="repl" select="'\r'"/>

      </xsl:call-template>

    </xsl:variable>

    <xsl:variable name="step5">

      <xsl:call-template name="replace">

        <xsl:with-param name="text" select="string($step4)"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="search" select="'	'"/>

        <xsl:with-param name="repl" select="'\t'"/>

      </xsl:call-template>

    </xsl:variable>

    <xsl:value-of select="string($step5)"/>

  </xsl:template>

  <!-- basic replace -->

  <xsl:template name="replace">

    <xsl:param name="text"/><xsl:param name="search"/><xsl:param name="repl"/>

    <xsl:choose>

      <xsl:when test="contains($text, $search)">

        <xsl:value-of select="substring-before($text, $search)"/>

        <xsl:value-of select="$repl"/>

        <xsl:call-template name="replace">

          <xsl:with-param name="text" select="substring-after($text, $search)"/>

          <xsl:with-param name="search" select="$search"/>

          <xsl:with-param name="repl" select="$repl"/>

        </xsl:call-template>

      </xsl:when>

      <xsl:otherwise>

        <xsl:value-of select="$text"/>

      </xsl:otherwise>

    </xsl:choose>

  </xsl:template>

</xsl:stylesheet>

 

The purpose of this XSLT mapping is to transform the XML structure coming from SAP into the JSON format required by the GraphQL service. More specifically, it extracts the GraphQL query string from the XML payload and embeds it into the query field of the JSON request body. Without this step, the GraphQL service would not accept the request, since it only supports application/json.

We import the XSLT mapping into PO as an imported archive.

 

 

Step 3 : Message Mappings

If there is no need for a custom graphical mapping, we perform a one-to-one mapping.

 

 

Step 4: Operation Mapping & Test

In the Operation Mapping, we place our one-to-one mapping as the first step, and then add a second step for the XSLT mapping.

We have successfully tested our XSLT mapping. For guidance on sending GraphQL requests with an application/json body, you can refer to the official GraphQL documentation or your server’s implementation guide;

https://learning.postman.com/docs/sending-requests/graphql/graphql-http/ 

Test XML : 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>

<ns0:MT_getFlightLegList_Req xmlns:ns0=********************************>

     <query>

<![CDATA[

Your GraphQL Request

  ]]>

</query>

</ns0:MT_getFlightLegList_Req>

 

 

Step 5: Integrated Configuration

Since PO does not have a GraphQL adapter, we will use REST. We create a standard REST Receiver adapter, and because the call from SAP will come via proxy, our sender is the SOAP XI adapter.

Rest Receiver Data Format Step:

Remember that we’ve converted the payload into the JSON format accepted by the service using XSLT. In other words, our adapter will receive a structure in JSON format.

We create the standard Integrated Configuration (ICO) and activate the scenario.

 

 

Step 6: Testing

You can use Send Message to test.

 

Monitoring:

Request BI Step:

After Mapping Step:

 

Response :

As the response includes personal data, I cannot share it. Nevertheless, this confirms that the request was successfully executed and a response was returned.

 

NOTE: There are scenarios where combining a Message Type with an External Definition in the same Service Interface prevents ABAP proxy generation in SAP. To mitigate this risk, ensure consistency by using the same artifact type (either Message Type or External Definition) on both request and response.

 

Thank you,

Deniz Z.

entry.title: Everything you need to move to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction - Why Now Is the Moment to Modernize Your SAP Integrations

Digital leaders are defined by the ease with which they connect people, data and processes. Yet many SAP customers still rely on SAP Process Integration/Process Orchestration (PI/PO) or SAP Cloud Integration (Neo environment)— platforms that have served well for years but were designed for a very different era of IT.

Today’s reality is hybrid and multi-cloud. Applications span on-premise data centres, hyperscalers and SaaS solutions; business models evolve in weeks, not years; and customers expect real-time, event-driven experiences. In this landscape, integration is no longer a back-office utility—it is the strategic backbone that powers innovation, agility and growth.

SAP Integration Suite is SAP’s answer to this new paradigm: a modern, enterprise-grade iPaaS that unifies API-led, event-driven and process integrations under one roof. With 200-plus connectors, AI-assisted tooling, and flexible deployment options across any major cloud, it’s purpose-built to help you:

  • Retire technical debt by moving from aging middleware to a future-proof platform.
  • Accelerate time-to-value with pre-packaged integration content and proven migration tools.
  • Operate hybrid at scale—bridge on-premise systems with cloud applications seamlessly.

This blog series is a practical guide for two key audiences:

  1. PI/PO customers planning a phased or full migration from on-premise middleware to the cloud.
  2. Neo-CPI customers ready to transition their integrations to the Cloud Foundry–based SAP Integration Suite.

We offer a guided approach to holistic integration that allows you to map your own unique journey by:

  • Assess your integration strategy while defining requirements
  • Leverage proven patterns for various integration scenarios and environments
  • Define integration best practices by adopting expert recommendations
  • Gain insights and adopt agile integration practices for continuous improvement

We’ll walk through preparation, tooling, migration steps, and best practices—backed by screenshots, tips from the product team, and lessons learned from early adopters. Whether you’re charting your first pilot or orchestrating an enterprise-wide move, you’ll find everything you need to modernise with confidence and speed.

Let’s get started.

 

  1. Connect SAP and Third-Party Systems Efficiently

Whether you're connecting SAP systems or integrating with third-party applications, SAP Integration Suite provides a comprehensive, enterprise-grade integration platform as a service (iPaaS). It supports application-to-application, business-to-business, and even business-to-government integrations using modern technologies like advanced API management. With prepackaged integrations, connectors, AI-powered tools, and business-centric APIs, you can get started quickly and scale integrations seamlessly — all while reducing manual effort and boosting efficiency.

 

  1. Accelerate Connectivity from Day One

Time-to-value is everything. SAP Integration Suite empowers you to kickstart your integration projects immediately. Thanks to its extensive library of prebuilt content, AI-supported advisors, and easily discoverable data semantics, you can reduce setup time dramatically and focus on what matters most: delivering business value.

 

  1. Enable Real-Time Business Interactions

In today’s digital landscape, real-time responsiveness is critical. SAP Integration Suite enables API-first and event-driven architectures that allow you to publish, consume, and process business data and events in real time — both within your organization and across your broader ecosystem. This means you can power real-time insights, automation, and digital experiences that drive meaningful outcomes.

 

  1. Integrate Hybrid and Multi-Cloud Landscapes Flexibly

Modern IT landscapes are hybrid and diverse. SAP Integration Suite is designed to operate across both on-premise and cloud environments. Whether you're working in a single public cloud, multiple clouds, or a mix of on-prem and cloud, SAP Integration Suite has the flexibility to adapt. It supports deployment on major public cloud infrastructures and offers runtime flexibility, so you can execute integrations where it makes the most sense for your business.

 

  1. Migrate with Confidence

Moving away from legacy systems doesn’t have to be risky or disruptive. SAP Integration Suite offers a clear, guided path to modern integration — supported by proven methodologies, helpful assets, and robust tools from SAP and its partners. This means you can migrate confidently, streamline operations, and unlock the full potential of your digital transformation initiatives.

Why move to SAP Integration Suite
As a technical developer responsible for SAP PI/PO, you’re increasingly constrained by its aging, on-premise architecture and the effort required to support modern, agile, and scalable integration patterns. SAP Integration Suite offers a future-ready platform that enables you to:

  • Modernize integration landscapes with cloud-native, microservice-based tools
  • Accelerate time-to-value through hundreds of pre-built integrations, APIs, and templates
  • Support hybrid and multi-cloud strategies with out-of-the-box connectors for SAP and non-SAP workloads
  • Embrace API-first and event-driven approaches for real-time responsiveness, reduced coupling, and easier extensibility

 

 Introductory Assets

Why do I need this assets?

Article: Producing tangible business benefits from modern iPaaS solutions by MIT Technology Review

Website: Smoothly transition to the cloud from SAP Process Orchestration

These introductory assets discuss why modern iPaaS solutions, like SAP Integration Suite, offer significant business benefits by streamlining and accelerating application integration through pre-built connectors, automation, and AI-enabled features for tasks like code analysis and testing, ultimately facilitating a smooth, strategic transition from legacy systems like SAP Process Orchestration while boosting agility and efficiency.

Technical Documentation

Why do I need this assets?

Migration Guide for SAP Process Orchestration

Integration Flow Design Guidelines

The technical documentation guides users through migrating from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite, covering assessment, migration steps, and best practices for designing integration flows in the new cloud-based environment, with a focus on connectivity, security, and error handling.

 Blogpost

Why do I need this assets?

Elevate from SAP Process Orchestration and Modernize your integration platform with SAP Integration Suite

Accelerate move to SAP Integration Suite with SAP Integration Suite Testing Service

These blog posts provide information about the strategic imperative and practical aspects of migrating from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite. They discuss the use of automated testing and migration tools to accelerate the transition, minimize risks, and ensure a smooth go-live.

Devtoberfest Sessions

Why do I need this assets?

Migrating to SAP Integration Suite with Int4 Shield

Successfully plan, prepare and execute your migration to SAP Integration Suite with Figaf

Partner Webinars:  https://events.sap.com/modernizationjourneywithpartners/en_us/home.html

 

These sessions focus on how to successfully plan, prepare, and execute a migration from SAP Process Orchestration, emphasizing the critical role of automated testing and migration tools like Int4 Shield and Figaf to reduce complexity, risk, and manual effort.

 Tutorials from SAP Tutorial Navigator

Why do I need this assets?

Use the Migration Assessment Application

How to migrate RFC Lookups from SAP Process Orchestration to Cloud Integration

Learn how to migrate JDBC Lookups from SAP Process Orchestration to Cloud Integration

Learn how to migrate SFTP Advanced File Selection from SAP Process Orchestration to Cloud Integration

These tutorials collectively teach how to leverage the Migration Assessment Application to plan and execute the migration of various integration scenarios (specifically RFC Lookups, JDBC Lookups, and SFTP Advanced File Selection) from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Cloud Integration, including practical steps and considerations for each type of migration.

 Learning Journey

Why do I need this assets?

Migrating to SAP Integration Suite


Developing with SAP Integration Suite

 

The learning journeys equip integration designers and developers with the knowledge and skills to successfully migrate integration scenarios from legacy SAP Process Orchestration/Process Integration to the modern SAP Integration Suite.

 Discovery Center Mission

Why do I need this assets?

Get Started with Migration to SAP Integration Suite

Automate the Migration from SAP Process Integration to SAP Integration Suite with Figaf 

These missions include best practices and step-by-step guidance for use case implementation, supported by mission experts and SAP Community.

 

Why move to SAP Integration Suite
As a technical developer using SAP CPI on the Neo environment, you face architectural and operational limitations that hinder modern integration demands. With SAP’s strategic shift to the Cloud Foundry-based SAP Integration Suite, now is the time to evolve and future-proof your integration capabilities:

  • Leverage a modern, flexible runtime based on Cloud Foundry with greater scalability and resilience
  • Access the full capabilities of SAP Integration Suite, including enhanced API management, event mesh, and open connectors
  • Simplify operations with improved lifecycle management, monitoring, and DevOps-friendly tooling
  • Adopt hybrid integration strategies with support for cloud, on-premise, and third-party systems

 

Introductory Assets

Why do I need this assets?

Free assessment for SAP BTP, Neo environment

 

Access this free service to assess your current SAP BTP, Neo environment implementation; plan your migration to the multicloud environment; and develop a migration plan.

Technical Documentation

Why do I need this assets?

SAP Cloud Integration Migration Guide

Use this guide to transfer your integration scenarios in the Cloud Integration tenant from the Neo environment to SAP Integration Suite in the Multi-Cloud Foundation.

Blogpost

Why do I need this assets?

Migrate from Neo to Cloud Foundry - SAP Cloud Integration

This blog highlights the process with some screen shots of migrating integrations from a Neo tenant to Cloud Foundry tenant

 Learning Journey

Why do I need this assets?

 

Modernizing Integration with SAP Integration Suite

This course offers a concise yet comprehensive introduction to SAP Integration Suite, covering the key capabilities, the importance of a defined enterprise-wide integration strategy, and how you can leverage AI to modernize your business landscape.

 

Thank you very much for tuning into the blogpost. We hope that those assets will help you to accelerate your journey to the next-gen integration platform SAP Integration Suite. Plese keep in mind that moving from PI/PO or Neo-CPI is not simply a ,,lift & shift" but it is a journey to a modern way to connect business processes and applications across your enterprise.

Big thanks to Kristin McMahon and Ryan Champlin for co-authoring this blogpost!

entry.title: Migrating from SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite (CPI): A Practical Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

As organizations move towards cloud-based integration platforms, migrating from SAP Process Orchestration (PO) to SAP Integration Suite (CPI) becomes essential. This blog will walk you through the key steps of this migration, focusing on:

  • Connecting SAP PO to SAP Cloud Connector And adding SAP PO in SAP Integration Suite (CPI)

  • Importing Message Mappings from SAP PO

  • Importing dependent objects of the Message Mapping,  such as Imported Archives and Function Libraries

 

‌‌ Step 1: Connecting SAP PO to Cloud Connector

Since SAP PO is an on-premise system, we use SAP Cloud Connector to securely connect SAP CPI (cloud) to the SAP PO environment. The Cloud Connector acts as a secure tunnel between cloud applications and on-premise systems without exposing them directly to the internet.

  1. Install SAP Cloud Connector  on the same network as SAP PO (for testing install on your system).

  2. Connect Cloud Connector to your SAP BTP subaccount (SAP CPI tenant).
    Establishing a Connection Between Cloud Connector and Subaccount 

  3. Login to the SAP Cloud Connector and select the subaccount and click on Cloud to On-Premises.

  4. Add the SAP PO System in Cloud Connector.

    Back-end Type: ABAP System
    Protocol: HTTP
    Internal Host: *PO Sytem Host Name without http://*
    Internal Port: *PO Sytem port*
    Virtual Host: *Enter a desired virtual hostname. This will be used when connecting from Integration suite.*
    Virtual Port: *Enter a desired port*
    Allow Principal Propagation: check✔️
    Principal Type:Kerberos
    Host in Request Header: Use Virtual Host
    Next ==> Next==>
    Check Internal Host: check✔️
    Finish

     

  5. Add resources path by clicking on the + icon and enter / select Path and all Sub-Paths ==> Save.

  6.  Check whether the added SAP PO system is reachable.

    If it's not, cross-check the host and port. Ensure that both the SAP PO system and the Cloud Connector are accessible through the same network.

  7.  Now we have successfully connected to SAP PO system from SAP Cloud Connector.

Step 2: Connecting SAP PO  to SAP Integration Suite(CPI) Through SAP Cloud Connector

 

  1. Login to the Integration suite, Go to settings ==> Integrations==> select System Tab==> click on +  to add the SAP PO Sytem.

  2. Enter the details of System Overview and Integration Directory.

    In the Address box, enter the Virtual Host and Port with http://, as maintained in the Cloud Connector.
    If your PO System uses the same address for both the Integration Directory and the ES Repository, check the corresponding checkbox✔️. Otherwise, specify them separately. 
  3. Test the connectivity between Integration suite and SAP PO system.

  4.  Now we have successfully connected to SAP PO system from Integration suite.

Step 3: Importing Message Mappings from SAP PO to SAP CPI

  1.  Login to the Integration suite and go to your package and click on Add message mapping. Selcet ES Repository and select the added SAP PO System and connect.

  2.  Select the required message mapping and click on next.

  3.   To import Message mappings there are some prerequisites:

    Based on your message mapping, you may have dependent resources such as a Function Library, Imported Archive, WSDL, or XSD. 

    WSDL and XSD files are imported automatically along with the message mapping. However, Function Libraries and Imported Archives must be imported separately.

    After selecting the message mapping, you will be able to see the list of dependent resources. You must import the required resources (Function Library and Imported Archive) first; otherwise, the message mapping cannot be imported successfully.

     

 

  1. Importing Dependent Objects: Function Libraries
    1. Create a function library artifact, click on "Upload," connect to the SAP PO system, select your function libraries, save, and then deploy.

    2.  Create a Imported Archives artifact, click on "Upload," connect to the SAP PO system, select your Imported Archives, save, and then deploy.

           4. Since the dependent objects have been imported, we can now import the message mapping. Add the message                               mapping artifact, select the ES Repository, choose the added SAP PO system, connect, select the message mapping,                     click "Next," and then select the dependent objects that were imported as separate artifacts.

5. Click on "Select" and choose the required function library artifact.

6. If the status shows "Already Existing," click on it and select "Reuse Existing Resource.

 

7. Next, click on "Select" and choose the required imported archive artifact. Then, click "Submit" to import the message      mapping.

 8.  Now the Message Mapping is successfully imported to Integration suite from SAP PO system.

 

Conclusion

Migrating from SAP PO to SAP CPI goes beyond a simple technical shift—it’s an opportunity to modernize integration landscapes, adopt cloud-first patterns, and leverage the flexibility of SAP BTP. By carefully setting up connectivity through the Cloud Connector, systematically migrating message mappings, and addressing dependencies like archives and function libraries, you can ensure a smooth and reliable transition.

A phased migration approach, combined with thorough testing and best practices, will help minimize risk and maximize value. This journey not only preserves existing investments but also sets the stage for future-ready integrations.

Stay tuned for my upcoming blogs, where I’ll deep dive into adapter migration strategies and lessons learned from real project experiences. 🚀

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite Deep Dive, Hands-on, and Customer Event (Oct 22, 2025 Palo Alto)
entry.content:
Our SAP Integration Suite team is hosting an event at our SAP office's in Palo Alto @ SAP Building 1 on October 22, 2025.  

Whether you are just considering SAP Integration Suite and have a lot of questions, are new to the technology and still getting your feet wet, or are a seasoned user of SAP Integration Suite there is value for you at this event.   We will have SAP Integration Suite product experts leading all sessions at this one-day event.  

We will provide a deep-dive into the capabilities of SAP Integration Suite, take a tour of the latest roadmap, provide an overview of migration tools, services, and the latest financial promotions to make the move, provide a hands-on experience of SAP Integration Suite, and provide time to sit one-on-one with a product expert to get your questions answered.  

During this SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) knowledge-exchange with our product management team, you will:

  • Gain a deeper understanding of SAP Integration Suite with a hands-on learning experience
    • SAP Integration Suite is a modern, scalable, and secure platform as a service (iPaaS), empowering businesses to integrate all applications and systems across SAP and non-SAP landscapes using one solution. It supports real-time data exchange, automation, and API-driven innovation, facilitating business processes across your entire ecosystem, including cloud, on-premise, and hybrid environments
  • Be inspired by local SAP Integration Suite customer Pacific Coast Building Products 
    • Speaker:  Miguel Carranza - SAP Applications Engineering Manager, Pacific Coast Companies
    • Session Title:  From Legacy to Leadership:  Pacific Coasts Journey to SAP Integration Suite
    • Key Topics:  Improve integration platform to be more supportable, flexible, and extensible.  Why SAP Integration Suite?  Migrations to SAP Integration Suite (IS) from legacy technologies like SAP Neo Environment (CPI) and Seeburger.  Benefits of SAP IS, how they are getting there, and what the future holds.
  • Dive into advanced use cases and capabilities, including:
    • Cloud Integration to build and run application-to-application (A2A), business-to-business (B2B), and business-to-government (B2G) scenarios with over 3,200 prebuilt integrations
    • Advanced Event Mesh to design, deploy, and manage an event-driven architecture (EDA) that enables real-time business processes at scale
    • Edge Integration Cell to support hybrid integration needs with a cloud or local runtime
    • API-Management to secure, govern, and transform your APIs with management and delivery processes that drive innovation while safeguarding against threats
  • Discover how to leverage the 2025 Transformation Incentive to save money on migration from SAP Process Integration, SAP Process Orchestration, or SAP Cloud Integration on the SAP BTP, Neo environment, and third-party solutions to integrate SAP and non-SAP systems

Register now and bring your laptop! Seats are limited for this hands-on event.

We look forward to seeing you on Wednesday, October 22, 2025, at SAP Building 1, 3410 Hillview Avenue, Palo Alto, CA 94304.

If you have any questions about the event please reploy to this post and we'll follow-up with you.

entry.title: New CI Pipeline Restart Extension features: supporting new Pipeline package and improved Restart Job
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the release of Cloud Integration Pipeline Extension - Restart via Data Store in version 1.0.1.

This update introduces several powerful new features and improvements:

  • Supports new integration packages for the Pipeline for Cloud Integration and allows flexible linkage of Generic Pipeline copies to the Pipeline Stages.
  • Enhanced Restart Job Integration Flow:
    • Generic Job Profile to restart all integration scenarios defined in the Partner Directory through the Pipeline Framework.
    • New configuration options to persist the Job Log.
    • New Integration Flow "Pipeline API - Restart Job Log" to retrieve both the Restart Job run list and detailed log data (if log persistence is enabled).
  • Improved configuration and monitoring: New parameters (such as "NotOlderThanHours" for Restart Job), several bug fixes, enriched monitoring headers, and better exception handling.

With these enhancements, version 1.0.1 delivers more flexibility, transparency, and control when managing restarts in your Cloud Integration pipelines.

Integration Package content

New package consists of six Integration Flows, one central Script Collection and one Value Mapping. 2 new artifacts have been added:

  • Pipeline API – Restart Job Log (Optional). This Integration Flow enables API-based retrieval of stored Job Logs. It is built on top of the standard Message Processing Logs (MPL) API and allows either to retrieve the restart job log or to query MPL details of executed Restart Jobs.
  • Value Mapping to determine Pipeline Stage (Optional). The Data Store Entry Name includes a 2-letter code for the Pipeline Stage, derived from the technical name of Pipeline IFlow where the error occurred. This Value Mapping allows to link custom IFlow copies to their Pipeline Stage.

Support for new Pipeline integration packages

The new Cloud Integration Pipeline packages introduce Integration Flows with updated technical IDs that include the version marker “v2”. To ensure compatibility, these new IFlow IDs have been added to the stage determination logic.

In addition, the latest Pipeline version now propagates the MPL ID of the source Pipeline IFlow to the custom error handling IFlow. This allows the Data Store entry name to be built using the original source MPL ID, rather than the MPL ID of the custom error handling flow (as was the case in version 1 of the Cloud Integration Pipeline package).

Pipeline Stage Determination

Each Data Store entry name includes the mandatory attribute Pipeline Stage, which indicates where the exception occurred. In the new version of the Restart Extension, the pipeline stage is resolved through a two-step process. If neither step succeeds, a default value is assigned: Stage (NA) or "Not Assigned".

  1. Technical ID Prefix Check. The system first evaluates the prefix of the source pipeline Integration Flow’s technical ID. This was the only approach available in version 1.0.0. For example, if you copy a generic Integration Flow to create a receiver-specific outbound pipeline IFlow, the technical ID must start with the correct stage prefix:
    com.sap.integration.cloud.pipeline.v2.generic.step06.outbound.myCustomIFlow is resolved to stage (OB).
  2. Value Mapping Check. If the stage cannot be determined from the prefix, the new version applies a Value Mapping. This mapping links custom IFlow technical IDs to specific pipeline stages, allowing you to flexibly assign non-standard technical IDs to the correct stage (see screenshot).

Generic (Default) Job Profile

In version 1.0.0, the Restart Job IFlow could restart only specific interfaces defined in the Job Profile. One of the most common feedback points was the need for a generic Restart Job, similar to what is available in SAP PO, that could restart all integration scenarios configured for the Cloud Integration Pipeline.

This capability has now been added. With the Generic Restart Job Profile, all scenarios where the Partner ID is determined using the Alternative Partner approach can be restarted in one go. The configuration effort is minimal: the only requirement is that the Job Profile status is set to "active."

Optionally, you can exclude specific integration scenarios from the Generic Restart Job by providing them in the parameter InterfacePidExclusionList.

New Job Profile Parameters

The Restart Job Profile now supports additional optional parameters that extend flexibility and monitoring:

  • NotOlderThanHours. In version 1.0.0, filtering was only possible with the static interval parameters TimeFrom and TimeTo. In the new version, if TimeFrom is not explicitly set, this parameter can dynamically calculate it as: 
TimeFrom = (Job run start timestamp) – (Configured number of hours in NotOlderThanHours)
  • RestartJobLog. By default, the job log is stored only in memory within the message header jobLogHeader (accessible only when Trace is activated). With this parameter, you can define where and how the job log is stored:
    • attach – the job log is attached to the MPL of the Restart Job IFlow, using the Restart Job ID as MPL Attachment name.
    • persist – the job log is persisted in the database using the Persist Message step.
    • custom – the job log is sent via a ProcessDirect extension to a custom-built IFlow. In this case, an additional parameter must be maintained in the Job Profile restartJobLogExitEndpoint with the value of ProcessDirect endpoint.

Pipeline API Operations

A new API Integration Flow Pipeline API – Restart Job Log has been introduced in the latest package version. This Integration Flow enables API-based retrieval of Restart Job Logs, provided that the RestartJobLog parameter of the Job Profile is set to either "attach" or "persist".

The following queries are supported:

  • Retrieve the restart job log for a specific Restart Job instance by supplying either the MPL ID of the Restart Job IFlow or the unique Restart Job ID.
  • Query MPL details of executed Restart Jobs based on selection criteria, in order to obtain the MPL ID of the Restart Job IFlow or the unique Restart Job ID.

For further details, see the updated Configuration Guide included in the package.

entry.title: Test Automation Solution for PO7.5 to SAP Integration Suite Migration
entry.content:
Test Automation Solution for PO 7.5 to SAP Integration Suite Migration

Enterprises are transitioning away from legacy SAP Process Orchestration (PO) towards SAP Integration Suite as PO approaches end of life. While this migration promises better scalability and integration capabilities, it also brings a significant challenge of ensuring data consistency and integrity throughout the process without disrupting business users.

In any migration project, testing can consume around 40% to 50% of the overall effort if done manually. Manual testing is time-consuming and prone to human error. Complex interfaces, multiple test cases, and large volumes of data drives up the time and cost of testing. Thoroughly validating that the new environment behaves as expected is critical to a successful transition. This is where automated testing solutions comes into play, simplifying and accelerating the validation of migrated interfaces.

 We have built a test tool to extract, compare, and validate payloads between your legacy PO system and SAP Integration Suite. This tool simplifies and automates testing of migrated iFlows, making your transition smoother, faster, and more reliable.

Key Features of Our Test Automation Tool

  • Automates payload extraction from SAP PO 7.5: Extracts input and output messages from your existing PO environment. It establishes connectivity with the PO System and pulls the payload for testing.
  • Validates test cases directly against migrated flows in Cloud Integration : Run test scenarios on the target platform(SAP Integration Suite) to verify correctness of migration.
  • Compares output payloads across systems: Detect any mismatches or errors.
  • Supports various testing approach: Whether testing single or multiple interfaces, Our tool adapts to project needs. It supports regression testing.
  • Generates detailed, downloadable test reports: Gain visibility into test results with comprehensive  reports.
  • Alerts via mails in case of failures: Immediate notifications enable quick corrective actions.
  • Supports standard adapters.
  • Monitoring through quality management and governance dashboard.

Whether you are migrating a few interfaces or hundreds, test tool brings efficiency, visibility, and reliability to your testing process saving both time and cost.

Integrating our test automation tool into your migration strategy will help with:

  • Faster testing cycles that reduce the overall migration timeline.
  • Improved reliability with consistent, repeatable automated test runs.
  • Greater visibility into testing outcomes through detailed reports.
  • Enhanced confidence that migrated interfaces behave exactly as expected.
  • Scalability to handle migrations involving just a few interfaces or hundreds.

Ready to accelerate your PO migration testing during migration? Reach out to learn how our tool can simplify your migration from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite and save time, reduce costs, and ensure a smooth migration.

entry.title: Master Blog: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tool and Guidance
entry.content:
Migration Journey Overview: From Assessment to Tooling

Migrating from SAP Process Orchestration to the SAP Integration Suite represents a significant milestone in your organization’s cloud transformation journey. This master blog serves as a central hub for comprehensive information and guidance related to the Migration Tool and the broader migration process. It provides links to detailed blogs, documentation, and best practices to help you successfully plan and execute your migration to SAP Integration Suite.

Before beginning any migration activity, it’s crucial to start with the Migration Assessment - a key capability of the SAP Integration Suite. The assessment analyses your on-premises landscape, including existing integration configurations, mappings, custom adapters, and dependent objects. It provides detailed insights and a comprehensive overview indicating which scenarios can be migrated as-is, which ones require optimization, and which may need to be redesigned or modernized. This step establishes a clear, data-driven foundation for a successful and efficient migration journey.

Once your Migration Assessment is complete, the next big step is moving your integration content to the cloud - and that’s where the Migration Tool comes into play!

The Migration Tool, built into SAP Integration Suite’s Cloud Integration capability, helps you bring your existing integration scenarios from SAP Process Orchestration to the cloud with ease. Think of it as your Guided workflow for migration - it identifies what can be moved, converts integration artifacts into their cloud-ready counterparts, and helps you streamline the entire process with minimal manual effort.

Migration Tool – Offerings and Supported Components

The Migration Tool comes packed with a set of features to make your move from SAP Process Orchestration to the SAP Integration Suite smoother and faster. Here’s an overview of the key components it supports and automates.

Connectivity to On-Premise Systems: Choose the appropriate connection setup for securely linking your on-premise landscape with the SAP Integration Suite. For detailed configuration options, see Configuring Connectivity to an SAP Process Orchestration System

Object Types: The tool supports migration of integration artifacts such as Integrated Configurations, which define how messages are processed within the Java-only runtime of SAP Process Orchestration systems.

It also supports Receiver Determination, which is available in SAP Process Integration dual-stack installations (from release 7.5 onwards) where message processing takes place on both ABAP and Java stacks.

 

Patterns: The Migration Tool supports a variety of integration patterns to help you transition your existing scenarios to cloud-ready designs. These patterns provide flexibility for different integration needs - whether it’s point-to-point communication, content-based routing, or complex orchestration flows.

For the complete list of supported patterns, refer to the Migration Patterns

Migration Approaches: The Migration Tool offers two approaches - Standard Approach and Pipeline Approach to help you move your integration scenarios efficiently to the cloud. For more details, see Pipeline Concept

 

Communication Channels: The Migration Tool helps move your existing communication channels to the cloud, making sure your integration endpoints and related settings are mapped correctly. A few exceptions may apply, so some channels might need minor adjustments after migration. For more details, see Standard Adapter Migration.

Adapter Module Support: The tool automatically migrates PGP encryption and decryption modules to cloud-compatible configurations. Additional adapter modules are provided through the community package and can be leveraged as needed. The following list provides an overview: Adapter Modules

Decoupling and Ordered Message Processing with JMS Queues: The Migration Tool supports JMS queue configurations to enable decoupled communication between senders and receivers. In SAP Integration Suite Cloud Integration, internal JMS Adapter help separate asynchronous message processing from the sender, allowing messages to be handled independently and reliably.

To ensure messages are processed in the correct order - EOIO, the Access Type should be set to Exclusive, which ensures that only one consumer/worker processes messages from the queue at a time.

Note: This configuration is not applied automatically by the Migration Tool and must be set manually.

Idempotent at Receiver: The tool also provides idempotent message handling at the receiver side. This means it helps prevent duplicate message processing, ensuring that each message is delivered and processed only once - an essential feature for maintaining data consistency.

Reusing Artifacts for Efficient Migration: The Migration Tool provides options for users to reuse existing standalone artifacts - message mappings and function libraries, to their migrated scenarios. This flexibility allows message mappings and other artifacts to be reused across multiple integration scenarios, reducing redundancy and speeding up the migration process.

Example: Enable Reusable Artifacts

 

Migration Summary: After the migration, a detailed summary report provides an overview of all successfully migrated Integration Flow Configurations and their related objects in the cloud. If any object could not be migrated, this information is clearly highlighted in the summary, helping you identify follow-up actions easily.

The Migration Tool does offer a downloadable Summary Report that captures essential migration details - including the on-premise system details, integration patterns used, chosen migration approach, and other key information related to each migrated object, which you can review later if required.

Note: If you’d like to select an alternative runtime profile instead of the default Cloud Integration, you can manually set it in the Integration Runtime configuration after the successful migration of the Integration Flow.

Error Logs: When the Migration Tool cannot migrate to certain integration scenarios, it generates a detailed error log summary. This log provides insights into the specific issues encountered, helping you understand what went wrong and take corrective actions.

Hint: The error log is available to download as a .csv file. You can import it in Excel and use a delimiter as Comma (,) to transform the data, to review and analyse the migration errors effectively check columns ‘Status’ and ‘Message’.

 

For detailed, step-by-step instructions on using the Migration Tool, refer to the official tutorial: Use the Migration Tooling

 

Known Limitations of the Migration Tool

While the Migration Tool covers a wide range of integration scenarios, there are some known gaps and limitations. For the most up-to-date information, refer to Known Limitations of Migration Tooling 

Disclaimer: The list of gaps and limitations may shorten as SAP expands coverage and implements features on its roadmap

 

Next in the Series

Stay tuned for the upcoming posts where we will dive deeper into specific migration scenarios, tips, and best practices to help you get the most out of SAP Integration Suite.

Published:

✅ Migrating Point-to-Point Asynchronous Scenarios Using the Migration Tool in SAP Integration Suite

✅ Migrating XSLT mappings from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite 

✅Migrating Content-Based Routing Scenarios Using the Migration Tool in SAP Integration Suite

Up Next:

➡️ Extended Receiver Determination using the Pipeline Approach

➡️Operation-Specific Receiver Determination and Multi-Operation Scenarios

➡️Content-Based Routing (CBR) – Synchronous Use Cases

 

 

References - SAP Documentation and Blogs:

Migration Guide for SAP Process Orchestration

Get Started with Migration to SAP Integration Suite

Assessment Blog: How to use the Migration Assessment Application to get ready to migrate your SAP Process Orchestration Scenarios?

Migration Tool: Migration tool in Cloud Integration Capability of SAP Integration Suite

entry.title: How to Implement Dynamic Throttling in SAP CPI Using Groovy Script
entry.content:
Introduction:

Managing API rate restrictions or avoiding backend system overloads are common problems in SAP Cloud Integration (CPI). When an API receives too many requests in a short period of time, some may reject them, while others may experience significant slowdowns.

In this post, I will demonstrate how to create a dynamic delay mechanism in CPI using Groovy Script, where the sleep duration varies according to request count(header value).

Problem Statement:

  • Rate-limiting is often necessary for backend or API services.
  • Standard CPI configuration does not offer a built-in dynamic sleep/throttle feature.
  • When the load increases, developers need a lightweight way to decrease request speeds.

Solution Approach:

We can use a Groovy Script in CPI to:

1 .Read a custom header (SAP_ApplicationID in this case).

2. Transform it into an integer request count.

3. Implement adaptive delay logic with different thresholds.

Proceed with processing after the determined sleep duration.

//Groovy script
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
 
def Message processData(Message message) {
 
    def body = message.getBody();
 
    // Read SAP_ApplicationID
    def headerValue = message.getHeader("SAP_ApplicationID", String.class);
    def requestCount = 0;
 
    if (headerValue != null) {
        try {
            requestCount = headerValue.toInteger();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            requestCount = 0; // Default if not a number
        }
    }
 
    // Determine sleep time 
    def sleepTime = 0;
    if (requestCount >= 3500) {
        sleepTime = 1500;
    } else if (requestCount >= 2000) {
        sleepTime = 1000;
    } else if (requestCount >= 1000) {
        sleepTime = 500;
    } else if (requestCount >= 500) {
        sleepTime = 200;
    } else if (requestCount < 500) {
        sleepTime = 100;
    }
 
    // Apply sleep delay
    if (sleepTime > 0) {
        sleep(sleepTime);
    }
 
    message.setBody(body);
    return message;
}

How it works :

Step 1: Get SAP_ApplicationID from headers.

Step 2: Convert it into an integer(requestCount).

Step 3: Decide sleep time dynamically:

  •  ≥ 3500 - 1500 ms
  •  ≥ 2000 - 1000 ms 
  •  ≥ 1000 - 500 ms
  •  ≥ 500 - 200 ms
  •  < 500 - 100 ms

Step 4: Sleep for calculated time.

Step 5: Pass the original message forward.

Use cases:

1. For high-frequency situations, API rate restriction with shorter delays works perfectly.

2. Backend overload protection with millisecond-level throttling.

3. Testing integrations under various load conditions.

Conclusion:

This Groovy script provides a simple but effective way to dynamically throttle requests in SAP CPI based on header values. By introducing adaptive delays, you can protect backend systems, comply with API rate limits, and simulate controlled load behaviour.

entry.title: Migrating Point-to-Point Asynchronous Scenarios Using the Migration Tool in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
This blog post is part of the series Master Blog: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tool and Guidance exploring how to migrate various integration patterns from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite using the Migration Tool. In this blog, we will focus on the Point-to-Point (P2P) Asynchronous pattern, which is one of the most common and fundamental integration scenarios.

 

Objective

The goal of this blog is to explain how the Migration Tool supports P2P asynchronous integrations, including:

  • Key configuration options and technical considerations
  • Best practices to ensure reliable and decoupled communication
  • How to implement JMS decoupling and Idempotent Processing effectively

 

Understanding the Point-to-Point Asynchronous Pattern

In a Point-to-Point (P2P) asynchronous integration, a message is sent from a sender to a receiver without a response message. This pattern promotes decoupling between systems, improves reliability, and allows retry mechanisms to ensure delivery in case you are using JMS queues. By default, the Migration Tool will enable JMS queues during migration.

If there are constraints on the limit of available JMS queues, the tool also supports direct communication without JMS, though this reduces decoupling and resilience. If you are migrating a high number of asynchronous interfaces, SAP recommends using the pipeline approach, which will not be covered in this blog. You can find more information in Pipeline Concept and  Migration Tooling now supporting migration via the pipeline approach.

 

Migration Tool Support for P2P Asynchronous Scenarios using Standard Approach

The Migration Tool automates most of the effort by generating a ready-to-use Integration Flow (iFlow) and the corresponding configuration objects in SAP Cloud Integration.

Depending on your settings, the tool can generate Integration Flows that:

1. Decouple with JMS Queue: Use JMS queues for decoupling between sender and receiver systems (only shown for Asynchronous interfaces):

When you select this option, the Migration Tool creates two Integration Processes within the generated iFlow:

  • Inbound Process
    • Receives the message from the migrated Sender Component and Communication Channel
    • Forwards the message to the JMS queue
  • Outbound Process
    • Reads the message from the JMS queue
    • Performs any required processing (e.g., message mapping, data transformation)

Delivers the message to the receiver system

Please note that you need to maintain the name of the JMS queue post-migration using the already created external parameters:

2. Idempotent Process at the Receiver Side: Use the idempotent process call if you want to make sure that the message will be delivered exactly once to the target system:

When you select this option, the generated iFlow will include an Idempotent Process Call. This mechanism ensures that a message is delivered exactly once to the receiver system, avoiding duplicate postings.

The API call at the receiver system is wrapped then into this local integration process.

There are receiver adapters that do supports idempotency such as SOAP RM, XI and IDoc. In these cases, it’s preferable that you don’t use the Idempotent option on Migration Tool and choose to handle it directly in the adapter. You can find more information in Special Use Cases: SAP RM vs XI vs IDoc.

 

Post-Migration Activities and Special Scenarios

While the Migration Tool significantly accelerates the transition from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite, a few manual fine-tuning steps are typically required.

Generic Post-Migration Steps

After migrating a P2P Asynchronous interface:

  1. Review and update external parameters for sender and receiver connectivity.
  2. Define the JMS queue name to be used in both Integration Processes (in case checkbox is marked during wizard). This can be done in the external parameters’ configuration view.
  3. Update
  4. Review if you want to change the value of the header SAP_Sender according to the naming convention that will be adopted on SAP Integration Suite.

Manual Activities Depending on Integration Scenario

You can also find below few examples of manual activities post-migration that needs to be performed depending on the content of your integration scenario:

  1. Functions like RFC Lookup or JDBC Lookup on Graphical Message Mapping are not automatically converted. Replace them with equivalent Integration Suite adapters or externalized APIs. Tutorials are available for JDBC lookups and RFC lookups.
  2. If your interface is using any custom code (e.g., UDF, Function Library, Java Mapping) or XSLT that have additional dependencies, Therefore, you need to export them from SAP Process Orchestration and import into your Integration Flow
  3. If your interface is using a fault message handling, this won’t be migrated using the Migration Tool. Therefore, you need to import the mapping using the standalone functionality and add an exception handling subprocess into the migrated iFlow

Please refer to the Known Limitations of Migration Tooling for additional insights into cases that may require manual adjustments after migration. we will explore specific topics in more depth, such as XSLT transformation differences between SAP Process Orchestration and SAP Integration Suite, custom code migration, fault message handling, and other advanced migration scenarios.

 

Async-Sync Bridge Support

The Point-to-Point Asynchronous pattern also covers scenarios using an Async-Sync bridge.

In SAP Process Orchestration, an asynchronous interface is converted into synchronous using the RequestResponseBean module, enabling a response message to be sent back to the sender asynchronously using a second interface object.

The Migration Tool can migrate this Async-Sync bridge scenario as well, ensuring that both asynchronous and synchronous steps are merged into a single iFlow.

Please note that the Sync-Async bridge scenario (the opposite from the above), is not supported as of today.

 

Conclusion

In this first blog of the blog series, we explored how to migrate Point-to-Point Asynchronous scenarios and how the Migration Tool simplifies this process by leveraging JMS decoupling and Idempotent Processing. These capabilities help ensure reliable, scalable, and fault-tolerant communication between systems.

entry.title: How to connect SAP PO running over netweaver 7.50 with OpenText XML Adapter
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's digital landscape, enterprises rely heavily on seamless integration between platforms to maintain agility, optimize operations, and enhance digital transformation initiatives. SAP Process Orchestration (SAP PO) is a comprehensive integration software that enables the synchronization of processes and data across numerous SAP and non-SAP applications. One such integration possibility involves connecting SAP PO with OpenText's SOAP API to facilitate efficient document management capabilities.

OpenText is a leading enterprise information management company known for its robust solutions, including its SOAP API which provides functionalities for document management. Integrating SAP PO with OpenText's SOAP API allows organizations to achieve a streamlined connection between their business processes and their document management systems, thereby enhancing access to vital information and documents.

Before embarking on this integration journey, several key considerations must be addressed, including authentication mechanisms, message format mapping, and error handling. Given that SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) relies on XML-based messaging protocols, mastering these elements is critical for a successful and secure integration. This article will guide you through the essential steps for establish a secure connection to OpenText's SOAP API and offer insights into how to authenticate users and manage documents effectively.

Goal

The goal of this article is to guide enterprises on connecting a legacy system to OpenText using SAP PO, and to generate a single endpoint that encapsulates multiple requests to different services exposed by OpenText. By achieving this, organizations can streamline their document management processes, reduce complexity, and enhance the efficiency of their operations.

 

The first step in order to achieve the integration of both systems will be to Establishing a secure and reliable connection between SAP PO and OpenText's SOAP API requires robust authentication mechanisms.

In this case we will delve into the basic authentication. Understanding this methodology is crucial for safeguarding your data and ensuring authorized access only.

Once authenticated, the core of the integration lies in the consumption of document management features offered by the OpenText SOAP API. This section offers a step-by-step guide on how to configure SAP PO to interact with OpenText's document management functionalities.

To simplify, the basic architecture will be structured this way.

A single interface is presented to the legacy system, which is then divided within SAP PI to engage multiple interfaces based on a custom tag referred to as method.

Step 1.

Generate a single data type with a common structure for the services that will be consolidated on one.

The following Data Type will be used as the request

And this Data Type will be used for the response

Transformation process involve several use of graphical mapping and XSLT transformations in order to achieve the goal to get a single entry point for all the exposed services, with the graphical mappings allowing to include transformations and validations in order to detect the data type received 

Step 2 

Define the graphical message mappings for request and response, one for each inbound service interface defined.

Step 3

At the response graphical mapping, add custom java functions in order to identify and set the data type of the values that are returning, the custom functions must to be embedded inside the mapping and be called as custom functions.

This custom function receive 5 entry parameters that are returned by the opentext get node method, evaluate the data type of the value and return a single text with the description of the type, the goal of this is reduce the complexity of the logic at the legacy system.

Here is the Java code of the custom function

 

at also another custom function was developed to validate the data type of any value returned at the Values tag

Step 4

Build the XSLT transformations in order to adapt the structure to the opentext services, this will add the corresponding namespaces, eliminate non necessary tags and generate the parents when will be need it.

Step 5.

Build the operation mapping that will be in charge of call sequentially the graphical message mapping and the XSLT transformations, this operation mapping will have one step for request and another for response considering the process defined as Synchronous.

Request side will at first call the graphical mapping and at second will use the XSLT transformation

The response side will call the XSLT transformation that will adapt the structure, then add the namespace and at last use the graphical mapping where the custom java functions are located

Once all the steps are done and the iFlow is created and deployed the service could be tested using SOAP UI.

 

Conclusion

This approach allow to generate a single endpoint to consume some specific methods of the Opentext implementation, allowing to get an homogeneous structure at response, but will require some work if new methods will be implemented, at also the architecture could be considered slightly coupled since maintenance could be required as well but not frequently.

The current architecture could face several challenges to be migrated to SAP Integration Suite running over BTP

entry.title: Partnering for Progress: Accelerate Your Move to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
When I first started working with SAP’s Migration Factory program, I quickly realized just how big of a shift many organizations were facing.
For years, SAP Process Integration (PI) and SAP Process Orchestration (PO) have been the backbone of countless businesses’ integration landscapes. But with mainstream maintenance ending in 2027, the question everyone’s asking is: “What’s next?”

And the answer is clear — SAP Integration Suite on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).
It’s modern, scalable, and built to meet the needs of today’s connected, cloud-first world. But let’s be honest — migration isn’t just about technology. It’s about people, collaboration, and having the right partners by your side.

That’s where Migration Factory 2.0 comes in.

Building a Stronger Ecosystem — Together

What excites me most about Migration Factory 2.0 is that it’s not just a framework — it’s a movement.
We’re bringing together SAP and our partners under one unified goal: to help customers modernize with speed, confidence, and shared expertise.

Through this program, partners get the enablement and resources they need to deliver migrations effectively, and customers get access to a trusted ecosystem that’s been trained, certified, and supported directly by SAP.

It’s truly a “win-win-win” — for SAP, our partners, and most importantly, our customers.

Behind the Program: Enablement, Execution, and Engagement

At its core, Migration Factory 2.0 is built on three simple but powerful pillars:

  • Enablement: We equip partners with the latest training, certifications, and migration tooling — including our Integration Suite Black Belt 2.0 program.

  • Execution: We support them with proven methodologies, automation frameworks, and best practices developed alongside SAP experts.

  • Engagement: We celebrate their success through co-marketing, events, and storytelling opportunities that amplify their impact across the SAP ecosystem.

Every one of these pillars is about empowering collaboration — because success in integration isn’t achieved in isolation.

Why Partners Are the Game Changers

Working closely with our partners, I’ve seen firsthand the dedication, innovation, and creativity they bring to every customer engagement.
Migration Factory 2.0 gives them more than just visibility — it gives them a platform to shine.

Through the program, partners can:

  • Earn SAP’s Partner Badge for SAP PO/SAP Integration Suite Modernization

  • Be featured on our Migration Factory Partner Listing on SAP.com

  • Participate in co-marketing campaigns, webinars, and video series like Integration Situation

  • Receive qualified migration assessments from SAP

  • Connect with other partners and SAP experts through enablement sessions and community collaboration spaces

It’s inspiring to see how quickly our partner community has embraced this journey — not just as a business opportunity, but as a shared mission to help customers move forward.

Helping Customers Choose the Right Partner

For customers, we’ve made it easy to find the right expertise through the Migration Factory Partner Listing on SAP.com.
Each partner listed has been enabled through SAP’s framework and offers migration assessments, readiness checks, or implementation services tailored to customer needs.

Whether you’re in Europe, Asia, or North America — you can now connect directly with a partner who understands your regional and industry-specific challenges.

My Favorite Part: The Collaboration

One of my favorite things about being part of this initiative is watching new partnerships form — partners collaborating with each other, learning from SAP experts, and even co-creating new migration approaches together.
It’s a reminder that technology may power transformation, but people make it happen.

Every week, I see stories from partners who have taken what they learned from Migration Factory enablement sessions and turned it into real customer impact. That’s what keeps me passionate about this program — it’s not theory, it’s transformation in action.

Let’s Move Forward — Together

If you’re a partner, now’s the time to get involved.
Join the Migration Factory 2.0 program, earn your certifications, and showcase your success stories to the global SAP community.

If you’re a customer, explore the Migration Factory Partner Listing on SAP.com to connect with certified experts who can help you plan your move from SAP PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite.

The future of integration is here — and it’s collaborative, connected, and cloud-driven.
Together, we’re accelerating integration modernization — one connection at a time.

entry.title: Migrating XSLT mappings from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
This blog post is part of the series Master Blog: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tool and Guidance exploring how to migrate various integration patterns from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite using the Migration Tool. In this blog, we will explore the migration process of XSLT mappings.   

While SAP Integration Suite, Cloud Integration supports XSLT mappings, you may encounter subtle yet impactful differences when migrating XSLT-based mappings from SAP Process Orchestration (PO). This post outlines the main variations between both environments, explains typical runtime issues, and provides practical guidance for smooth adaptation.

 

XSLT Processor Differences 

The XSLT processor behind Cloud Integration is Saxon EE 9.9.1.6 (as of November 2025 – see this Blog for details on verifying the processor version) while SAP Process Orchestration next to its default allowed the import and use of other processors. 

While these differences should not usually cause major compatibility problems, certain manual adjustments after the tool-assisted migration might be required to deploy or execute mappings successfully. In particular, there are variations in how standard XSLT functions handle data types, how Java classes are used within XSLT code and how other stylesheets are included. 

 

Data Type Handling in XSLT Functions 

SAP Process Orchestration tends to be more lenient regarding data type usage in standard XSLT functions. Cloud Integration, on the other hand, applies stricter type validation during compilation and runtime. 

Example 1: Using xsl:sort with Data Types 

The xsl:sort function sorts data based on specified criteria: 

<xsl:sort select="data" data-type="text"/> 

The allowed data types for this function are "text" and "number". On SAP Process Orchestration, the following syntax works without error: 

<xsl:sort select="DATES" order="ascending" data-type="xsd:Date"/> 

However, in Cloud Integration this causes a runtime error: 

Adjusting the data-type accordingly to text or number solves this issue. However, one needs to pay attention to the effects this might have on the format and thus the rest of the stylesheet execution. 

 

Example 2: Using format-number() with non-numeric inputs 

On SAP Process Orchestration, the following syntax works without error: 

<xsl:value-of select="format-number(substring(data, 0, 3), '0.00')"/> 

In Cloud Integration, this causes a compilation error during runtime: 

The issue arises because format-number() requires a numeric input, while substring() returns a string. 
To resolve this, explicitly convert the substring to a number: 

<xsl:value-of select="format-number(number(substring(data, 0, 3)), '0.00')"/> 

Please note that if a global number format is defined, this conversion might affect the formatting of the substring according to that global setting. 

 

Using Java Classes in XSLT 

XSLT mappings sometimes rely on custom Java classes to extend the functionality of native XSLT functions, a common practice in integration projects where XSLT offered fewer built-in capabilities.  

During migration, you may encounter Java classes ranging from simple helpers (e.g., string or date transformations, counters) to more complex ones (e.g., document loaders, RFC calls, or value mapping lookups). While some advanced use cases may require redesign or alternative approaches, simpler helper classes can still be used in Cloud Integration. 

Step 1: Uploading the Java Classes 

Upload the Java classes as a JAR file and add them to the iFlow’s References (using the Archive option). 

If the java classes are contained in the same Imported Archive (IA) as the XSLT using them on PO, the migration tool will automatically import them as it imports the entire IA during the migration process. However, if the java classes are in a separate IA, this needs to be done manually post tool migration. 

Please note that the JAR file must be added as a local resource into the iFlow. Currently it’s not possible to use as a reusable artifact. 

Step 2: Adjusting XSLT Syntax 

 

  1. Namespace Declarations 

SAP Process Orchestration allows multiple styles for Java namespace declarations, while Saxon (the processor in Cloud Integration) supports only one format. 

  1. a) Inline Declaration 

xmlns:java="http://xml.apache.org/xslt/java" 

<xsl:variable name="date" select="java:com.edi.xslthelper.DateTransform.GetDate(data)"/> 

  1. b) Namespace Declaration with Class Path 

xmlns:date="java:com.edi.xslthelper.DateTransform" 

<xsl:variable name="date" select="date:GetDate(data)"/> 

In Cloud Integration, only the second option (namespace declaration with class path) is supported. Therefore, all Java class calls must be manually adapted accordingly. 

 

  1. Type Handling in Java Class Calls 

Just like native XSLT functions, Java class calls in Cloud Integration require explicit type consistency. For example: 

<xsl:variable name="__function4" select="variable:setValue($var,'Name',string(number(data)))"/> 

If a Java class expects a string, the input must be explicitly converted. On SAP Process Orchestration, the same code might work without this conversion. 

In one specific migration case, a Java method required an integer input — here, neither round() nor number() worked, but the XSLT function xs:integer() was necessary. 
 

Please note that compilation errors such as “Errors were reported during stylesheet compilation” are often related to incorrect data types. Explicitly converting inputs usually resolves these issues. 

 

References to other stylesheets within the XSLT 

XSLT code from different stylesheets can be used in an XSLT mapping using the include function: 

<xsl:include href="Stylesheet2.xsl"/> 

The included stylesheet needs to be added manually as a reference to the iflow: 

On Cloud Integration, no folder structures are supported so if the IA on PO had a nested folder structure containing the stylesheets, make sure to upload them individually and remove the path in the include function of your original stylesheet like our example above. 

 

Recommendations for Successful Migration 

To ensure smooth execution of XSLT mappings in Cloud Integration: 

  • Always verify and explicitly define correct data types. 
  • Adapt namespace declarations for all Java class calls. 
  • Adapt the include of additional stylesheets. 
  • Upload all necessary additional components (jar files, stylesheets). 
  • Test mappings individually after migration to detect type-related issues early. 
  • Simplify or refactor complex Java-dependent logic where possible. 

Following these guidelines helps prevent runtime errors and ensures stable, predictable execution after migration. 

 

Conclusion and Next Steps 

Migrating XSLT mappings from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite requires close attention to how the underlying XSLT processor interprets data types and Java extensions. 
While most mappings migrate seamlessly, subtle differences, particularly in data type handling, namespace declarations, and custom Java integrations, may lead to compilation or runtime issues if not adjusted. 

Stay tuned for the next part of this series, where we’ll continue our deep dive into migration patterns and modernization best practices in SAP Integration Suite, including insights on content-based routing, custom code handling, and error management.

entry.title: FAQ – Migration from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
This blog provides answers to the most frequently asked questions from customers and partners planning their migration from SAP Process Orchestration (PI/PO) to SAP Integration Suite.

This content was prepared by many experts from different areas at SAP and it covers assessment, tooling, technical migration topics, and operational aspects to help you plan and execute a smooth transition to SAP’s cloud-native integration platform.

Last Updated: Nov 2025 – SAP Integration Suite Move Experts.

 

Assessment and Planning

This section is focused on questions related to the initial planning or assessment of your landscape, normally before the start of the migration project.

 

Q1: Why should I move from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite?

A: Migrating from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite enables organizations to modernize their integration landscape with a cloud-native, fully managed, and future-proof integration Platform as a Service (iPaaS) that supports both cloud and hybrid scenarios.

It eliminates infrastructure maintenance, reduces total cost of ownership, and accelerates time to value through more than 4000 prebuilt integrations, 4,600+ APIs, and approx. 1400+ business events available on SAP Business Accelerator Hub (as of end of 2025) and keep adding up. See more information in Value Proposition: Why You Should Move to SAP Integration Suite.

 

Q2: What is the Migration Assessment tool in SAP Integration Suite?

A: The Migration Assessment is a capability within SAP Integration Suite designed to analyze your SAP Process Orchestration interfaces providing many insights about the interfaces’ complexity, dependencies, categories of functionalities and effort estimation for the technical migration. See more information in Migration Assessment.

 

Q3: Which SAP Process Integration/Orchestration versions are supported by the Migration Assessment?

A: The Migration Assessment supports SAP Process Integration/Orchestration versions:

  • 7.31 SP28 and above
  • 7.40 SP23 and above
  • 7.50 SP06 and above

The Migration Assessment supports interfaces created based on Interface Configuration Object (ICO) and interfaces created based on Receiver Determination (together with Sender Agreement, Receiver Agreement and Interface Determination) in dual stack environments.

Note: For 7.50 (SP06 to SP09), make sure to have implemented the patch as described in the SAP Note 2456339.

 

Q4: How can I start planning my migration project?

A: Begin by running the Migration Assessment to understand your system landscape and estimate migration efforts. Then define the migration strategy (e.g., phased migration, modernization on focus areas).

Review the Migration Guide, evaluate reusable content, and prepare your SAP Integration Suite tenants (development, test, and production). SAP recommends the Discovery Center mission Get Started with Migration to SAP Integration Suite to get confidence and understanding on the tools.

 

Q5: I have SAP Process Integration 7.1. Can I use the Migration Assessment and/or Migration Tool?

A: SAP cannot guarantee that the Migration Assessment and Migration Tool can work properly in an SAP Process Integration version different than the ones supported (see question 3), although many of the APIs used might be available on the same. SAP recommends upgrading the SAP Process Integration to a supported release before running the assessment and/or migration to ensure accurate results.

 

Q6: How can I determine the number of required message blocks while moving from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite?

A: In the Migration Assessment result, you can have the counting of message throughput and some performance indicators per interface such as the average message size, for the previous month that you ran the extraction. You can use both information to calculate the number of required messages blocks in SAP Integration Suite.

Make sure to activate the monthly performance collection on SAP Process Orchestration as per SAP Note 3300008.

For synchronous messages, you might need to check if synchronous messages are logged on the Message Overview Monitor as per the SAP Note 2442373. Make sure to read throughout the note and agree or not with any additional overhead due to the additional message logging in case this is not enabled, and you want to collect also such data.

For more information of what is the definition of a Message on SAP Integration Suite refer to the SAP Note 2942344 and Discovery Center.

 

Q7: Where can I find more information about how to use the Migration Assessment?

A: You can find more information about the Migration Assessment in the following references:

  • Use the Migration Assessment Application
  • How to use the Migration Assessment Application to get ready to migrate your SAP Process Orchestration Scenarios?

 

Q8: I have on-premise to on-premise integration scenarios and I don’t want to exchange my messages over the Internet in SAP Integration Suite. What is the best solution?

A: For on-premise to on-premise integrations where you don’t want messages to enter the public Internet, the best solution is to run your integration flows using the Edge Integration Cell. It lets you design and manage integrations in the cloud while executing all runtime processing inside your own network, fully avoiding external message exchange. You can find more information in the following references:

  • What Is Edge Integration Cell
  • Deployment Models
  • Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) on Edge Integration Cell
  • 3247839 - Prerequisites for installing SAP Integration Suite Edge Integration Cell

 

Q9. What are the typical migration approaches and how do they differ?
A: Typical migration approaches are:

  • Lift-and-Shift: Move existing SAP Process Orchestration interfaces to SAP Integration Suite with minimal functional changes. This reduces short-term project risk and effort, preserving legacy designs (quick win) and safeguarding existing investments. Use when timelines are tight or when business logic must remain unchanged. The downside is to lose the momentum to promote innovation leveraging other SAP Integration Suite capabilities than Cloud Integration.
  • Modernization: Re-design interfaces to use API-led or event-driven patterns for instance, remove deprecated technologies, consolidate redundant interfaces, centralize common functionalities and promote a Clean Core mindset. This requires more design work but yields long-term maintainability, scalability and ensures a future-proof landscape.

 

Q10. How long does a typical migration project take, and what resources are required?
A: Duration depends on landscape size, complexity, and chosen approach:
